attributes.stem:
A previously well 45-year-old woman presents with a two week history of post-prandial upper abdominal pain. She has no other symptoms and does not use any medications. On examination her blood pressure is 118/64 mmHg, pulse 70/min and temperature 37.0oC. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.

As part of the diagnostic workup, an abdominal ultrasonography is performed. Nothing abnormal is found, apart from a 3 cm mass in the right adrenal gland. There are no concerning features such as haemorrhage, calcifications, or irregular margins.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This adrenal lesion is almost certainly an incidental finding. Incidental findings on imaging are relatively common. Of otherwise fit and healthy adults having an abdominal CT scan, perhaps some 50% will have an incidental finding on the imaging. In 25% the finding will be of potential clinical significance.

Incidental adrenal masses are well recognised, with an incidence increasing to about 7% in patients over the age of 70. Most adrenal incidentalomas (85%) are benign, with the remaining 15% associated with malignancy or hormone hypersecretion (Cushing's, Conn's, phaeochromocytoma). Small lesions (<2 cm) and those with no radiological features of malignancy can be kept under review once it has been established that there is no evidence of endocrine dysfunction.

Therefore in this case the first step is to rule out the presence of a functional (hormonal producing) adrenal mass. The dexamethasone suppression test is used to assess adrenal gland function, particularly in cases of suspected hypercortisolism (Cushing's syndrome). Even though this patient has no history of hypertension, diabetes, or endocrine abnormalities, and the mass is incidentally discovered without evidence of hormone overproduction, an overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test should be performed. This should be the priority in this patient's management.

As part of the assessment of adrenal gland function, a 24-hour urine fractionated catecholamines test should be ordered to assesses for phaeochromocytoma by measuring metabolites of catecholamines. Elevated levels suggest adrenal medullary tumour activity. Importantly, catecholamines are the metabolites of adrenaline and noradrenaline which are the primary hormones secreted from phaeochromocytomas - both adrenaline and noradrenaline have very short half lives and therefore are not useful for testing directly. Symptoms of phaeochromocytoma such as hypertension, palpitations, or sweating are not present in this case, making phaeochromocytoma less likely.

Some further investigations will likely be subsequently required to rule out malignancy. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography plays a pivotal role in distinguishing benign from malignant adrenal tumours. Benign adenomas typically exhibit well-defined borders, homogeneous density, and rapid washout of contrast material during the venous phase. Conversely, malignant lesions often present with irregular borders, heterogeneous enhancement, increased vascularity, calcifications and delayed contrast washout. Additionally, size (>4 cm), necrosis, invasion into surrounding structures, and lymphadenopathy raise suspicion of malignancy.

Biopsy of adrenal mass is generally not recommended due to the risk of complications such as bleeding, the potential for needle-track seeding of malignant cells and the possibility of obtaining nondiagnostic samples. Additionally, a biopsy may not definitively differentiate between benign and malignant lesions. These risks must be carefully considered, especially in asymptomatic patients with incidentally discovered adrenal masses. Therefore, adrenal mass biopsy is not typically the initial step in managing an incidentaloma.

Repeating the scan after six months is not the most appropriate next step in managing this incidentaloma due to several reasons. Firstly, the incidental mass seen on ultrasonography warrants a more accurate diagnostic evaluation with a CT scan to look for adverse features like density and enhancement patterns. Secondly, hormonal studies are required. Also, waiting for six months could prolong the patient's discomfort and anxiety.
learningPoints: When incidental adrenal mass is detected, the first goal is to distinguish between benign and malignant masses, and the second goal is to find out its functional status (hormonally active or inactive), to decide further management.

CECT features suggestive of adrenal tumour malignancy include irregular borders, heterogeneous enhancement with a high attenuation value (typically > 10 Hounsfield units), necrosis, invasion into surrounding structures, and lymphadenopathy.
references: Gendy R, Rashid P. Incidental adrenal masses A primary care approach. Aust Fam Physician. 2017; 46:385-90. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2017/june/incidental-adrenal-masses-a-primary-care-approach

Mahmood E, Loughner CL, Anastasopoulou C. Adrenal Adenoma. [Updated 2023 Aug 17]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539906/

Jackson BS. Adrenal Incidentaloma Controversial Size Recommendations. Urol Res Pract. 2023 Mar;49(2):96-99. doi: 10.5152/tud.2023.22245. PMID: 37877855; PMCID: PMC10192718. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10192718/
Hu J, Kassu R, Titanji B, et al. Evaluation of Adrenal Incidentaloma. Surg Clin N Am 2024; 104:837–49. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38944503/
Kebebew E. Adrenal Incidentaloma. N Engl J Med 2021; 384:1542-51. Available at: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMcp2031112

attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)

userDetails.name: George Balalis
userDetails.title: Dr
userDetails.position: Bariatric, Upper GI and General Surgeon

attributes.instructions: Hi George, we would be very grateful for your review of this item that Uday has written on an adrenal incidentaloma. Thank you for your help.

Hi team, just looking over guidelines for management of adrenal incidentalomas - as per these 2017 RACGP guidelines determining functional status (Dex suppression and urine metanephrines) is recommended to be carried out before contrast CT imaging. Just wondering if this is your experience as well or if there are alternative guidelines to follow. I've seen a few questions on this topic so following confirmation I'll go through the rest to ensure consistency. Cheers, Jess

attributes.title: Dr
attributes.name: George Balalis
attributes.position: Bariatric, Upper GI and General Surgeon
